text: In this video, we present a case of total robotic total mesorectal excision with high vascular tie for rectal cancer. This was a fifty-seven-year-old lady without any significant comorbidities. The patient was positioned to modify clitatomy. Four robotic ports and one air cell port were used for the procedure.

In this video, we present a case of total robotic total mesorectal excision with high vascular tie for rectal cancer. This was a fifty-seven-year-old lady without any significant comorbidities. The patient was positioned to modify clitatomy. Four robotic ports and one air cell port were used for the procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral border of the duodenum, a key step that facilitates the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein, crucial for achieving tension-free colorectal anastomosis after radical rectal resection.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral border of the duodenum, facilitating the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein. The vein is divided at the inferior border of the pancreas, and dissection continues superiorly and laterally through the bursa omentalis, with careful separation of the pancreas from the left mesocolon.

The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior border of the pancreas and continues dissection superiorly and laterally through the bursa omentalis, carefully separating the pancreas from the left mesocolon. After completing the superior dissection, a peritoneal incision is made to dissect the inferior mesenteric artery, which is dissected up to its origin, ligated, and divided. The dissection of the rectum begins once the colonic part is completed.

Following the completion of the superior dissection, the surgeon makes a peritoneal incision to access the inferior mesenteric artery. The artery is dissected up to its origin, ligated, and then divided. The dissection then progresses to the rectum after completing the colonic portion.

The surgeon completes posterior dissection and proceeds with lateral and anterior mobilization. Following total mesorectal excision, the specimen is transected at the level of the anal canal and removed. A double-staple colorectal anastomosis is then performed.

The surgeon completes posterior dissection and proceeds with lateral and anterior mobilization. Following the total mesorectal excision, the specimen is transected at the level of the anal canal and removed. A double-staple colorectal anastomosis is then performed.
The operation performed without incompletion. The postoperative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged on postoperative day four.
